Modern Web Guidance

Web Ai

Web Development

Ai Coding

AI Agent

5 min read
Just now

--

Why AI-assisted development defaults to outdated patterns, and how a collaborative cross-browser standard is bringing the modern web platform to your IDE.
If you have used AI coding assistants like Claude, Copilot, or Gemini recently, you have likely felt the thrill of generating an entire user interface in seconds. But if you analyze the code your AI assistant produces, you will notice a frustrating trend.
Despite all their computational power, AI coding agents consistently default to older, outdated solutions.
They will readily write fifty lines of complex, custom JavaScript to build a scroll animation, handle an accordion, or establish positioning for a tooltip. Meanwhile, modern CSS and HTML already have native, high-performance APIs designed to handle those exact problems out of the box.
Why does this happen, and how can we fix it? The answer lies in a major cross-browser collaboration supported by the Google Chrome team, the Microsoft Edge team, and the web development community: Modern Web Guidance.

The AI Training Gap: Why Agents Write Bloated Code
AI models do not write legacy code because they are “lazy.” They do it because of how they are trained.
Large Language Models (LLMs) learn from vast bodies of public code. Because the web has existed for over three decades, public repositories are overwhelmingly saturated with legacy code patterns and outdated stack answers.
Even if an AI model technically “knows” that a modern browser API exists, it often lacks the density of real-world, modern implementation patterns required to generate clean, production-ready code. Consequently, it defaults to the most common answers in its training data, which usually translate to bloated JavaScript libraries or decade-old CSS workarounds.
Modern Web Guidance bridges this gap.

What is Modern Web Guidance?
At its core, Modern Web Guidance is an expert-curated set of agent skills that embed modern web platform expertise, best practices, and browser compatibility data directly into your coding agents.
Instead of letting an AI guess how to solve a problem, this tool inserts targeted, token-efficient guidelines directly into the agent’s context window. It acts as a real-time, expert “advisor” to your IDE assistant, steering it toward solutions that leverage modern, native platform capabilities.
The guidance covers over 100 web development use cases across several core disciplines:

• User Experience & Motion: Smoothly animating entry and exit states, styling custom scrollbars (scrollbar-color), establishing View Transitions, and creating physics-based easing.

• CSS & Layout: Transitioning to modern layout features like container queries, CSS subgrid, and utilizing highly responsive color spaces like oklch.

• Forms & UI: Building accessible forms with native validation styling (like :user-invalid), auto-sizing text input fields (field-sizing: content), and positioning tooltips natively with CSS Anchor Positioning.

• Performance: Breaking up heavy, long-running JavaScript tasks with scheduler.yield, optimizing Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) image priorities, and deferring heavy rendering with content-visibility.

• Built-in AI & Security: Accessing on-device client models directly for language translation, detection, or summarization (using Prompt and Summarizer APIs), and implementing secure Passkey authentication.

The Secret Weapon: “Baseline” and Intelligent Fallbacks
One of the biggest concerns with adopting cutting-edge web features is browser compatibility. AI models notoriously make outdated suggestions that fail to take project requirements or real-world browser support into account.
Modern Web Guidance solves this by integrating with Baseline, a standard that categorizes web platform features into three clear support tiers:

• Limited availability: The feature is not yet fully interoperable across browsers.

• Newly available: The feature recently became interoperable across all major browsers (within the last 30 months).

• Widely available: The feature is fully interoperable and has been for at least 30 months.
By default, if you do not configure a Baseline target for your project, Modern Web Guidance will default to Baseline Widely available. However, you can configure your agent to target a different Baseline, such as newly-available, simply by adding a couple of lines of configuration to your project's AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md file:
# AGENTS.md
web_platform:
baseline: newly-availableWhen your project targets newer features that are not yet universally supported, the tool instructs your AI agent to write nuanced, lightweight fallbacks. Instead of pulling in massive third-party polyfills, it forces the AI to write progressive enhancements, allowing modern browsers to run the native code while conditionally loading tiny, case-specific fallbacks (typically under 50 lines of code) for legacy environments.

Getting Started
Because Modern Web Guidance is built as an open agent skill, it is compatible with popular AI workflows and coding clients, including Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, JetBrains WebStorm, Gemini CLI, and Antigravity.
To install it and run the interactive setup wizard, you can run the following command directly in your terminal:
npx modern-web-guidance@latest installThis runs an interactive setup that configures the skills to your preferences.
If you just want to explore the guides yourself before committing, you can search the library locally using the CLI:
# Search for how to handle scroll snap highlights
npx modern-web-guidance@latest search \"carousel scroll snap\"The CLI will output the relevant use-case IDs, which you can then retrieve directly to view the raw markdown guidelines in your terminal:
# Retrieve a specific guide
npx modern-web-guidance@latest retrieve \"animate-to-from-top-layer\"
Proven Performance
Does it actually work? Early automated evaluations using a Playwright-based testing harness show a 37 percentage point improvement on average in the AI’s adherence to modern best practices. Across popular tools, the unguided-to-guided accuracy uplift was striking, with Claude Code seeing a +35 percentage point increase (climbing from 52% to 87%) and Antigravity improving by +33 percentage points (climbing from 54% to 87%).
Rather than relying on outdated, bloated frameworks, developers can confidently let their agents leverage native platform capabilities, resulting in faster load times, cleaner codebases, and much better user experiences.

Get the Latest Updates: Join the Early Preview Program
Modern Web Guidance is currently in active development. If you want to stay on the absolute cutting edge, bring the latest standards to your coding workflow, and help shape the project’s direction, you can join the official Early Preview Program directly.
For those who want to contribute guides or report agent bugs, you can also check out the official open-source Modern Web Guidance GitHub repository.
